summary: German artist, leading figure of the New Objectivity movement (with George Grosz). Known for the Metropolis triptych, Seven Deadly Sins, Portrait of Sylvia von Harden, and the war print series Der Krieg. His works were labeled 'degenerate art' by the Nazis.

works.name: Metropolis Triptych (1928)
works.description: Triptych depicting Weimar Germany nightlife. Central panel: Alfred Schulze plays saxophone in a nightclub with a dancing couple and a woman with pink ostrich feathers. Side panels: crippled WWI veterans begging, ignored by prostitutes wearing fur. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Central panel shows a saxophonist/brass band in a nightclub; side panels show crippled WWI veterans being ignored by prostitutes
examples: A saxophonist leans towards a dancing couple in the central panel; left panel shows a WWI veteran with two wooden legs begging for money
depicts crippled veterans of World War I being ignored by prostitutes wearing fur

works.name: Seven Deadly Sins (1933)
works.description: Allegorical painting. A child-like Envy wears a Hitler mask while riding the back of old hag Avarice; a skeleton with a scythe forms a swastika shape with the figures.

clues.clue: Child-like Envy wears a Hitler mask riding the back of hag Avarice; skeleton with scythe; figures form a swastika shape
examples: a child-like Envy wears a mask of this person while riding the back of the old hag Avarice next to a scythe-wielding skeleton
on the back of a bent-over hag rides a child in pale yellow wearing a mask with a toothbrush mustache; figures form the shape of a swastika

works.name: Portrait of the Journalist Sylvia von Harden (1926)
works.description: Unflattering portrait: monocled woman with bobbed hair in a red-and-black checkered dress smoking at a café table. Recreated in the film Cabaret. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Monocled woman with bobbed hair, red-and-black checkered dress, smoking at a café table
examples: a monocled woman in a red-and-black checkered dress smoking
a female journalist with bobbed hair, a red-and-black checkered dress, and a monocle smokes while crossing her legs at a cafe table

clues.clue: Recreated in two split-second shots in the film Cabaret
examples: Two split-second shots in this film recreate a painting of a monocled woman

works.name: Der Krieg (The War) — Print Series
works.description: Series of 51 war prints (also called cycle of woodcuts) showing WWI horrors. Modeled on Goya's Disasters of War. Includes scenes like Shock Troops Advance under Gas and Soldier and Nun.

clues.clue: Series of 51 war prints modeled on Goya's Disasters of War, including Shock Troops Advance under Gas
examples: this macabre series of woodcuts showing subjects like stormtroopers advancing under a gas attack and a skull infested with maggots
a cycle of 51 war prints modeled on this horrific Goya series of 82 prints

comprehensive_summary:
Otto Dix (1891-1969) was a German painter and leading figure of the New Objectivity movement alongside George Grosz. His work harshly depicted the social inequality and moral decay of Weimar-era Germany, as well as the horrors of World War I.

His Metropolis triptych (1928) shows a nightclub scene in the central panel with Alfred Schulze playing saxophone, a dancing couple, and a woman with pink ostrich feathers, while the side panels depict crippled WWI veterans begging for money, ignored by prostitutes in fur. Seven Deadly Sins (1933) is an allegorical work in which a child-like Envy wears a Hitler mask while riding the back of the old hag Avarice; a skeleton with a scythe completes the composition, and the figures form the shape of a swastika.

His Portrait of the Journalist Sylvia von Harden (1926) is an unflattering depiction of a monocled woman with bobbed hair in a red-and-black checkered dress smoking at a café table; this image was recreated in the film Cabaret. Der Krieg (The War) is a series of 51 war prints modeled on Goya's Disasters of War, including scenes like Shock Troops Advance under Gas and a skull infested with maggots.

Other works include Die Skatspieler (deformed veterans playing cards), The Trench (which sparked national controversy), and a portrait of Maud Arizona/Suleika prominently featuring tattoos. His paintings were labeled 'degenerate art' by the Nazis.
recursive_suggestions: New Objectivity — movement Dix co-founded with George Grosz
George Grosz — collaborator and co-leader of New Objectivity
